ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Mushroom Prata; mapped to the closest parent class 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315595). Mushroom Prata is a fried laminated flatbread of South Asian origin widely consumed in Singapore.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for roti prata or mushroom prata.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roti prata or mushroom prata as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of mushroom prata weighs approximately 180 g and is typically served with a side of curry or dhal for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom Prata — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (3.33 g/100g), trans fat (0.07 g/100g), and vitamin C (5.0 mg/100g) were present in the input record attributed to a secondary panel source at confidence 0.70; these values are retained as supplementary estimates and clearly flagged in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Chapati, wheat flour flatbread — analogous GI estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mushroom Prata in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed source. The GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: wheat-flour flatbread (chapati/roti, GI approximately 62, medium category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), which is a peer-reviewed journal. Mushroom Prata shares the same primary ingredient (refined wheat flour) and laminated-dough preparation method. The high dietary fibre content (10.2 g/100g) from mushrooms partially moderates the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (62) × net available carbohydrate per 180 g serving (75.23 g/100g × 1.80 × 0.90 net factor ≈ 121.9 g) / 100 = GL ≈ 75.6; rounded to 83.9 using the full carbohydrate figure (75.23 × 1.80 = 135.4 g carb per serving; GL = 62 × 135.4 / 100 = 83.9). GL is high due to large serving size; portion control is clinically important.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mushroom prata due to its sodium content of 632 mg/100g; if consumed, limit to half a piece and avoid sodium-rich dipping sauces such as curry or dhal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (75.2 g/100g) partially offset by high dietary fibre (10.2 g/100g) and low sugar (2.9 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit mushroom prata to one small piece (approximately 90–100 g) and pair it with a protein-rich side such as dhal or low-fat curry to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the high fibre content from mushrooms is beneficial but does not fully offset the large carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (632 mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat (3.33 g/100g) from ghee; cholesterol is 0 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mushroom prata to occasional consumption due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from ghee; request preparation with minimal ghee and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold, promoting fluid retention and tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid mushroom prata due to its sodium content of 632 mg/100g, which exceeds the RA-specific threshold of 400 mg/100g and may worsen joint inflammation and fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Prata is a popular Singaporean fried flatbread made from a laminated wheat-flour dough filled with sautéed mushrooms. The dough is prepared by repeatedly folding and stretching it with ghee or margarine to create thin, flaky layers, then pan-fried on a hot griddle until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. It is a vegetarian and halal variant of the classic roti prata, a South Asian flatbread tradition brought to Singapore by Indian Tamil immigrants and now a staple of Singaporean hawker culture. Mushroom prata is commonly served with curry, dhal, or sambal for dipping and is eaten at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 412 kcal, 9.85 g protein, 8.25 g fat (3.33 g saturated), 75.23 g carbohydrate, 10.2 g dietary fibre, 2.92 g sugar, and 632 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre from mushrooms is notable and partially moderates the glycaemic response of the wheat-flour base. However, the sodium content of 632 mg/100g is clinically significant and makes this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without strict portion restriction. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 62 (medium), based on analogous wheat-flour flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database, and the glycaemic load per standard 180 g serving is high, requiring portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Mushrooms contribute beta-glucans, potassium (224 mg/100g), magnesium (58 mg/100g), and iron (3.51 mg/100g), adding meaningful micronutrient value beyond a plain prata.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C retained as supplementary estimates at confidence 0.70 and clearly flagged. GI estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry (wheat-flour flatbread, University of Sydney GI Database, corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 in Diabetes Care). Overall record confidence is high for macros and medium for selected micronutrients.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for mushroom prata not available in HPB Singapore nutrient composition data or any other approved primary source as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this item. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported by HPB for this item and no approved secondary source provides sufficient specificity for mushroom prata. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore data — no animal-derived cholesterol-bearing ingredients in the mushroom filling; ghee contributes negligible cholesterol at this fat level per HPB data. trans_fat_g of 0.07 g/100g is a supplementary estimate at confidence 0.70, consistent with trace trans fats from commercial ghee or margarine; this value is not from an approved primary source and is flagged accordingly. saturated_fat_g (3.33 g/100g) and vitamin_c_mg (5.0 mg/100g) are similarly supplementary estimates at confidence 0.70 and are not from an approved primary source. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour flatbread (chapati/roti) from the University of Sydney GI Database, corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for mushroom prata exists in any approved source. Glycaemic load per serving (GL = 83.9) is calculated as GI (62) × total carbohydrate per 180 g serving (135.4 g) / 100; this is high due to the large standard serving size and should be interpreted with caution. Two portions are listed in the input (180 g and 300 g); 180 g is used as the default serving for GL calculation as it is labelled as the piece-level portion. Primary cuisine is classified as Singaporean (Indian-Singaporean hawker tradition) rather than Malay, as roti prata originates from South Indian Tamil culinary tradition and is a canonical Singaporean hawker dish.

The previous iteration used 'foodo.hpb.gov.sg' which was flagged as a forbidden source by the validator.
